# 基因技术知识引擎 (genetech.tools)

> 全球基因技术前沿知识引擎. 550 entities across 10 categories.
> Last updated: 2026-06-25

## API Endpoints

- /api/entities.json — Complete flat entity list (550 entities)
- /api/data.json — Full structured data with categories
- /api/graph.json — Knowledge graph (nodes + edges)
- /sitemap.xml — All indexable URLs

## Complete Entity Data

### biotech companies

#### CRISPR Therapeutics

- **name**: CRISPR Therapeutics
- **category**: biotech_companies
- **ticker**: CRSP
- **focus**: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing
- **key products**: Casgevy (exa-cel) - approved SCD/TDT therapy
- **hq**: Switzerland
- **founded**: 2013
- **development stage**: commercial
- **key achievements**: First CRISPR therapy approved (Casgevy), Sickle cell and beta-thalassemia treatment
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Intellia Therapeutics

- **name**: Intellia Therapeutics
- **category**: biotech_companies
- **ticker**: NTLA
- **focus**: In vivo CRISPR gene editing

- **hq**: USA
- **founded**: 2014
- **development stage**: clinical
- **key achievements**: First in vivo CRISPR editing in humans, LNP delivery platform

#### Cargo Therapeutics

- **name**: Cargo Therapeutics
- **category**: biotech_companies
- **ticker**: CRGX
- **focus**: Next-generation CAR-T cell therapy — enhancing cell therapy efficacy
- **key products**: CRG-022 — CD22 CAR-T for relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies, CRG-023 — dual-targeting CD19/CD22 CAR-T
- **hq**: San Mateo, CA, USA
- **founded**: 2022
- **development stage**: clinical
- **key achievements**: IPO in November 2023, raising ~$280M, Developing enhanced CAR-T therapies with overexpression of key proteins to improve persistence, CRG-022 Phase 2 trial shows promising results in post-CD19 CAR-T relapsed patients, Spun out from Stanford University research
- **sources**: Cargo Therapeutics, SEC filings, ASH
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Metagenomi

- **name**: Metagenomi
- **category**: biotech_companies
- **ticker**: MGX
- **focus**: Discovering novel gene editing systems from metagenomic mining
- **key products**: MGX-001 — programmable nuclease for in vivo gene editing, MGX-002 — base editor platform, Meta Novo platform — AI-driven metagenomic discovery

- **founded**: 2016
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **key achievements**: IPO January 2024, raising ~$94M, Discovered novel CRISPR-Cas systems, recombinases, and integrases from environmental metagenomes, Partnership with Moderna for in vivo gene editing therapeutics, Pipeline includes programs for cardiovascular, metabolic, and neurological diseases
- **sources**: Metagenomi, SEC filings, Nature
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

- **key findings**: CRISPR gene editing market valued at USD 4.46 billion in 2025, projected USD 4.95 billion in 2026, Market expected to reach USD 13.39 billion by 2035, Approximately 250 clinical trials involving gene-editing therapeutics as of Feb 2025, More than 150 trials specifically involve CRISPR-based approach...

#### Stanford AI-Powered CRISPR Design

- **name**: Stanford AI-Powered CRISPR Design
- **category**: crispr_applications
- **application type**: AI-enhanced CRISPR design
- **description**: AI model that accelerates CRISPR guide RNA design and predicts editing outcomes for faster gene therapy development
- **key features**: Machine learning predicts gRNA efficiency, Faster gene therapy design cycle, Reduced off-target prediction, Automated optimization

#### In Vivo CRISPR Editing 2026 Wave

- **name**: In Vivo CRISPR Editing 2026 Wave
- **category**: crispr_applications
- **application type**: in vivo gene editing
- **description**: Major shift from ex vivo to in vivo CRISPR editing with ~250 clinical trials tracked by 2026, using LNP and AAV delivery

#### Casgevy

- **name**: Casgevy
- **application**: Gene therapy
- **target**: Sickle cell disease, Beta thalassemia
- **crispr system**: CRISPR-Cas9
- **status**: Approved (US, UK, EU, Switzerland, Canada, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, UAE)
- **description**: Casgevy is the first FDA-approved CRISPR-Cas9-based gene therapy. It treats sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia by editing patients' hematopoietic stem cells. The therapy has shown significant clinical success and represents a major milestone in gene editing therapeutics.
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-approves-first-gene-therapies-treat-patients-sickle-cell-disease', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'http...

#### Base Editing

- **name**: Base Editing
- **application**: Gene therapy
- **target**: DNA (small edits)
- **crispr system**: Base editing
- **status**: Clinical trials
- **description**: Base editing is a CRISPR technology that allows for precise small edits to DNA without double-strand breaks. It is being used in clinical trials to correct specific genetic variants. Researchers are currently dosing patients in trials for various genetic disorders.
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crispr-clinical-trials-2026', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12094669', 'collecte...

#### Prime Editing

- **name**: Prime Editing
- **application**: Precision genome editing
- **target**: DNA (precise genetic modifications)
- **crispr system**: Prime editing
- **status**: Research and clinical development
- **description**: Prime editing is an advanced CRISPR technology that enables precise genetic modifications without inducing double-strand breaks. It allows for a wide range of edits, including small insertions, deletions, and base changes. It is being explored for correcting specific variants in diseases like SCID.
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-025-01463-8', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12094669', 'collected_at': '2026-...

#### CRISPR Medicine News Clinical Trials Register

- **name**: CRISPR Medicine News Clinical Trials Register
- **application**: Clinical trial tracking
- **target**: Gene-editing therapeutic candidates
- **crispr system**: Various (CRISPR-based)
- **status**: Active monitoring
- **description**: This register tracks the advancement of CRISPR-based gene-editing technologies in clinical development. As of February 2025, it monitors approximately 250 clinical trials. Blood disorders, particularly sickle cell disease, lead the field with the majority of Phase 3 trials.
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://crisprmedicinenews.com/clinical-trials', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

- **name**: SCID Gene Correction
- **application**: Gene therapy
- **target**: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID)
- **crispr system**: Base and Prime Editing
- **status**: Preclinical studies
- **description**: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing, including base and prime editing technologies, is being used in preclinical studies to correct specific genetic variants causing SCID. These advanced editing approaches aim for precise corrections without the risks associated with traditional methods.

#### FDA Individualized Gene Editing Pathway for Rare Diseases

- **name**: FDA Individualized Gene Editing Pathway for Rare Diseases
- **application**: Regulatory framework
- **target**: Ultra-rare genetic diseases
- **crispr system**: CRISPR-Cas9, base editing, prime editing
- **status**: Active (2025-2026)
- **description**: The FDA has established a new regulatory pathway designed to accelerate the development of individualized (N-of-1) gene-editing therapies for patients with ultra-rare genetic diseases. This pathway aims to remove regulatory red tape for bespoke therapies that would otherwise be economically unviable...
- **companies**: FDA
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'A', 'article_url': 'https://www.clinicaltrialsarena.com/news/gene-editing-takes-centre-stage-in-fdas-new-rare-disease-approval-pathway', 'collected_at': '2026-06-22T06:40:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://ww...

#### VERVE-102 — Base Editing for Hypercholesterolemia

- **name**: VERVE-102 — Base Editing for Hypercholesterolemia
- **target genes**: PCSK9
- **target diseases**: Familial Hypercholesterolemia, Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease
- **editing type**: base_editing
- **development stage**: clinical_trial
- **companies**: Verve Therapeutics
- **mechanism**: Adenine base editing (ABE) to introduce a loss-of-function mutation in PCSK9 gene via LNP delivery, permanently lowering LDL cholesterol
- **key findings**: First-in-human base editing trial for cardiovascular disease (Heart-2 trial), LNP-mediated liver-targeted delivery of ABE mRNA and sgRNA, Single IV infusion designed to permanently turn off PCSK9, Preclinical data showed >90% PCSK9 editing in non-human primates with sustained LDL reduction, Potentia...
- **sources**: Verve Therapeutics, Nature Medicine, clinicaltrials.gov
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Prime Editing for p47phox-Deficient Chronic Granulomatous Disease (PM359)

- **name**: Prime Editing for p47phox-Deficient Chronic Granulomatous Disease (PM359)
- **target genes**: NCF1 (p47phox)
- **target diseases**: Chronic Granulomatous Disease (p47phox-deficient
- **editing type**: prime_editing
- **development stage**: clinical_trial
- **companies**: Prime Medicine
- **mechanism**: Prime editing to correct the GT deletion mutation in NCF1 gene, restoring functional p47phox protein in phagocytes
- **key findings**: First prime editing therapy to enter clinical trials (IND cleared 2024), Ex vivo editing of autologous hematopoietic stem cells, Preclinical: >50% correction efficiency with no off-target effects detected, Addresses autosomal recessive p47phox-deficient CGD, affecting ~25% of CGD patients
- **sources**: Prime Medicine, FDA, Nature Biotechnology
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### BEAM-302 — Base Editing for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency

- **name**: BEAM-302 — Base Editing for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency
- **target genes**: SERPINA1
- **target diseases**: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency (AATD)
- **editing type**: base_editing
- **development stage**: clinical_trial
- **companies**: Beam Therapeutics
- **mechanism**: Adenine base editing to correct the Z mutation (Glu342Lys) in SERPINA1 gene, restoring normal AAT protein production in liver
- **key findings**: BEAM-301/302 program entered clinical development 2024-2025, LNP-delivered base editors targeting liver hepatocytes, Preclinical: 60-70% correction of Z allele in primary human hepatocytes, Potential to address both liver and lung manifestations of AATD
- **sources**: Beam Therapeutics, Nature, clinicaltrials.gov
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### CRISPRoff/CRISPRon — Programmable Epigenome Editing

- **name**: CRISPRoff/CRISPRon — Programmable Epigenome Editing
- **target genes**: DNMT3A/3L (methylation writer)
- **target diseases**: Various genetic diseases, Cancer, Neurological disorders
- **editing type**: epigenome_editing
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **companies**: UCSF, Prime Medicine, Chroma Medicine
- **mechanism**: Dead Cas9 (dCas9) fused with DNA methyltransferase (CRISPRoff) or demethylation machinery (CRISPRon) to reversibly silence or activate genes without cutting DNA
- **key findings**: Published in Cell 2021, with significant therapeutic applications developed through 2025, Can silence most genes in the genome including those not previously targetable, Heritable silencing maintained through cell divisions without continued Cas9 expression, Reversible: CRISPRon can reactivate silen...
- **sources**: Cell, UCSF, Nature Biotechnology
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Casgevy Real-World Outcomes — CRISPR for Sickle Cell Disease

- **name**: Casgevy Real-World Outcomes — CRISPR for Sickle Cell Disease
- **target genes**: BCL11A
- **target diseases**: Sickle Cell Disease, Beta-Thalassemia
- **editing type**: CRISPR_editing
- **development stage**: approved
- **companies**: Vertex Pharmaceuticals, CRISPR Therapeutics
- **mechanism**: Ex vivo CRISPR-Cas9 editing of autologous hematopoietic stem cells to disrupt BCL11A erythroid enhancer, reactivating fetal hemoglobin (HbF) production
- **key findings**: First CRISPR therapy ever approved (FDA Dec 2023, UK Nov 2023), Real-world data 2024-2025: 96.5% of patients free from vaso-occlusive crises for ≥12 months, CLIMB-121 trial: 94% of SCD patients achieved complete response, CLIMB-131 trial: 100% of TDT patients became transfusion-independent, Long-ter...
- **sources**: NEJM, FDA, Vertex Pharmaceuticals, ASH 2024
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

### gene delivery

#### Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV) Vectors

- **name**: Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV) Vectors
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: viral vector
- **mechanism**: Non-integrating viral vector for gene delivery
- **key features**: Low immunogenicity, Long-term expression, Multiple serotypes for tissue targeting, Non-integrating
- **applications**: Gene therapy (Luxturna, Zolgensma), In vivo gene delivery, Retinal diseases, Neuromuscular disorders
- **development stage**: clinical/commercial
- **companies**: Spark Therapeutics, Novartis, RegenxBio
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Lipid Nanoparticles (LNPs)

- **name**: Lipid Nanoparticles (LNPs)
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: non-viral
- **mechanism**: Lipid-based nanoparticle encapsulation for nucleic acid delivery
- **key features**: Low immunogenicity, High delivery efficiency, Scalable manufacturing, Validated by mRNA vaccines
- **applications**: mRNA vaccine delivery, CRISPR component delivery, siRNA delivery, In vivo gene editing
- **development stage**: clinical/commercial
- **companies**: Moderna, Pfizer, Intellia Therapeutics
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Extrahepatic LNP Targeting

- **name**: Extrahepatic LNP Targeting
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: non-viral
- **mechanism**: Engineered LNPs for delivery beyond the liver
- **key features**: Organ-specific targeting, Modified lipid composition, Ligand conjugation
- **applications**: Lung delivery, Brain delivery, Spleen targeting, Tumor targeting
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: medium
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Precision LNP Design (Penn Engineering)

- **name**: Precision LNP Design (Penn Engineering)
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: non-viral
- **mechanism**: Redesigned LNP components for improved mRNA delivery precision
- **key features**: Redesigned ionizable lipids, Improved tissue specificity, Enhanced expression control
- **applications**: Targeted gene therapy, Precision medicine delivery
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **companies**: University of Pennsylvania
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: medium
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Adenoviral Vectors

- **name**: Adenoviral Vectors
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: viral vector
- **mechanism**: DNA virus vector for transient gene expression
- **key features**: High transduction efficiency, Large cargo capacity, Transient expression, Pre-existing immunity concern
- **applications**: Vaccines (J&J COVID), Cancer gene therapy, Oncolytic virotherapy
- **development stage**: clinical/commercial
- **companies**: Janssen, AstraZeneca
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Lentiviral Vectors

- **name**: Lentiviral Vectors
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: viral vector
- **mechanism**: Integrating retroviral vector for stable gene expression
- **key features**: Stable integration, Large cargo capacity (~8kb), Infects dividing and non-dividing cells
- **applications**: CAR-T cell engineering, Ex vivo gene therapy, Stem cell modification
- **development stage**: clinical/commercial
- **companies**: Kite Pharma, Novartis, Bluebird Bio
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### LNP vs AAV Debate (2026)

- **name**: LNP vs AAV Debate (2026)
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: comparison
- **mechanism**: Emerging comparison of LNP and AAV for in vivo gene therapy
- **key features**: LNPs gaining ground for in vivo editing, AAV established for gene addition, Redosing possible with LNPs, AAV has durability advantage
- **applications**: In vivo gene therapy, Gene editing delivery
- **development stage**: evolving
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: medium
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

- **mechanism**: LNP delivery of genes for diabetes treatment without viral vectors

#### AAV9

- **name**: AAV9
- **type**: Viral Vector
- **capacity**: 4.7 kb
- **tropism**: Broad, including central nervous system, skeletal muscle, and liver
- **advantage**: Low immunogenicity, long-term expression, FDA-approved for spinal muscular atrophy
- **status**: Clinical/Approved
- **description**: AAV9 is a widely used serotype of adeno-associated virus vectors, known for its ability to transduce various tissues, including the central nervous system. It has been successfully employed in gene therapies like Zolgensma for spinal muscular atrophy. Its minimal pathogenicity and ability to establi...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://scholars.duke.edu/person/Aravind.Asokan/publications', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-024-01780-w', 'collected_...

#### Lentivirus

- **name**: Lentivirus
- **type**: Viral Vector
- **capacity**: 8-10 kb
- **tropism**: Broad, can be pseudotyped for specific targeting
- **advantage**: Large cargo capacity, integrates into host genome for stable expression
- **status**: Preclinical/Clinical
- **description**: Lentiviral vectors are derived from retroviruses and are capable of transducing both dividing and non-dividing cells. Their primary advantage is the ability to deliver large genetic payloads and achieve long-term, stable expression through genomic integration. They are commonly used in ex vivo gene ...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.frontierspartnerships.org/journals/acta-biochimica-polonica/articles/10.3389/abp.2026.15740/full', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### Adenovirus

- **name**: Adenovirus
- **type**: Viral Vector
- **capacity**: 36 kb
- **tropism**: Broad, high transduction efficiency in many cell types
- **advantage**: Very large cargo capacity, high transduction efficiency
- **status**: Clinical/Approved
- **description**: Adenoviral vectors are known for their high transduction efficiency and ability to accommodate large genetic inserts, making them suitable for vaccines and gene therapies. However, they can elicit strong immune responses, which may limit their use for repeated administration. They have been successf...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://faseb.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1096/fj.202502611RR', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### AAV serotypes

- **name**: AAV serotypes
- **type**: Viral Vector
- **capacity**: 4.7 kb
- **tropism**: Varies by serotype (e.g., AAV1, AAV2, AAV5, AAV8)
- **advantage**: Versatile tropism, low immunogenicity, long-term expression
- **status**: Clinical/Approved
- **description**: Different AAV serotypes exhibit unique tropisms, allowing for targeted delivery to specific tissues. For example, AAV1 is effective for muscle, while AAV8 has a strong affinity for the liver. This versatility makes AAV a highly adaptable platform for developing gene therapies for a wide range of dis...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.packgene.com/learning-center/advancing-aav-gene-therapy-integrated-cmc-strategy-for-the-new-era-of-scalable-manufacturing', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'articl...

#### SORT LNP (Selective Organ Targeting Lipid Nanoparticles)

- **name**: SORT LNP (Selective Organ Targeting Lipid Nanoparticles)
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: non-viral vector
- **mechanism**: Engineered LNPs with adjustable surface charge (via SORT molecules) that redirect delivery to specific organs: lungs (positive), spleen (neutral), liver (negative)
- **key features**: Tunable organ selectivity by adjusting SORT molecule ratio, Non-viral — avoids anti-capsid immunity, Lower manufacturing cost than AAV vectors, Repeat dosing possible due to low immunogenicity
- **applications**: Lung-targeted mRNA delivery for cystic fibrosis, Spleen-targeted delivery for immune cell modification, Liver-targeted gene editing for metabolic diseases
- **advantages**: Tissue-specific targeting, Scalable manufacturing, Redoseable
- **limitations**: Transient expression, Lower efficiency than viral vectors for some tissues
- **companies**: ReCode Therapeutics, Generation Bio
- **development stage**: clinical
- **sources**: Nature Materials, PNAS, ReCode Therapeutics
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Engineered AAV Capsids (AAV-PHP.eB, AAVrh10)

- **name**: Engineered AAV Capsids (AAV-PHP.eB, AAVrh10)
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: viral vector
- **mechanism**: Synthetically evolved AAV capsids with enhanced tropism for specific tissues — AAV-PHP.eB crosses blood-brain barrier, AAVrh10 targets heart and skeletal muscle
- **key features**: AAV-PHP.eB: Systemic IV delivery to CNS, crossing BBB in mice, AAVrh10: Cardiac and skeletal muscle tropism, Engineered capsids evade pre-existing anti-AAV antibodies, Created by directed evolution (CREATE platform)
- **applications**: CNS-targeted gene therapy (AAV-PHP.eB), Cardiac gene therapy for heart failure, Whole-body gene delivery for systemic diseases
- **advantages**: Tissue-specific tropism, Reduced off-target delivery, Potential for immune evasion
- **limitations**: PHP.eB efficacy varies between species (mouse vs human), Manufacturing complexity
- **companies**: Dyno Therapeutics, Voyager Therapeutics, Sarepta
- **development stage**: preclinical/clinical
- **sources**: Nature Neuroscience, Cell, Dyno Therapeutics
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Exosome-Mediated Gene Delivery

- **name**: Exosome-Mediated Gene Delivery
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: non-viral vector
- **mechanism**: Engineered exosomes loaded with genetic cargo (mRNA, siRNA, DNA) using surface modifications for cell-type-specific targeting
- **key features**: Natural biocompatibility — low immunogenicity, Crosses biological barriers including BBB, Can be engineered with targeting ligands, Intrinsic tissue homing properties
- **applications**: Neurological disease (BBB-crossing exosomes), Cancer-targeted siRNA delivery, mRNA vaccine delivery
- **advantages**: Natural origin, BBB penetration, Low toxicity, Tissue tropism
- **limitations**: Loading efficiency challenges, Scalability of production, Heterogeneity of preparations
- **companies**: Codiak BioSciences, Capricor Therapeutics, Evox Therapeutics
- **development stage**: preclinical/clinical
- **sources**: Nature Nanotechnology, Science Translational Medicine
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Integrase-Mediated LNP Delivery (Sleeping Beauty, piggyBac)

- **name**: Integrase-Mediated LNP Delivery (Sleeping Beauty, piggyBac)
- **category**: gene_delivery
- **delivery type**: non-viral vector
- **mechanism**: LNP-delivered transposon systems (Sleeping Beauty, piggyBac) enabling stable genomic integration of therapeutic genes without viral vectors
- **key features**: Stable integration without viral components, Larger cargo capacity than AAV (>100kb vs ~4.7kb), Lower insertional mutagenesis risk than lentivirus, Cost-effective manufacturing
- **applications**: CAR-T cell manufacturing (non-viral), Hemophilia B gene therapy (factor IX), In vivo gene integration for monogenic diseases
- **advantages**: Large cargo capacity, Lower cost, Stable expression, Non-viral
- **limitationsations**: Integration site less predictable, Lower efficiency than viral in some tissues
- **limitations**: Integration site less predictable, Lower efficiency than viral in some tissues
- **companies**: Poseida Therapeutics, Aldevron, MaxCyte
- **development stage**: clinical
- **sources**: Nature Biotechnology, Molecular Therapy
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

### gene editing tools

#### CRISPR-Cas9

- **name**: CRISPR-Cas9
- **category**: gene_editing_tools
- **editing type**: knockout/knock-in
- **mechanism**: RNA-guided double-strand break via Cas9 nuclease
- **key features**: PAM: NGG, High efficiency, Widely validated
- **applications**: Gene knockout, Gene knock-in, Transcriptional regulation
- **development stage**: mature
- **companies**: CRISPR Therapeutics, Editas Medicine, Intellia Therapeutics
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### CRISPR-Cas12a (Cpf1)

- **name**: CRISPR-Cas12a (Cpf1)
- **category**: gene_editing_tools
- **editing type**: knockout
- **mechanism**: RNA-guided DSB with staggered cuts via Cas12a
- **key features**: PAM: TTTV, Staggered cut, Smaller guide RNA
- **applications**: Multiplex editing, Diagnostics (DETECTR), Gene knockout
- **development stage**: mature
- **companies**: Broad Institute, Mammoth Biosciences
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### CRISPR-Cas13

- **name**: CRISPR-Cas13
- **category**: gene_editing_tools
- **editing type**: RNA targeting
- **mechanism**: RNA-guided RNA cleavage via Cas13
- **key features**: Targets RNA not DNA, Collateral cleavage activity, No permanent genome changes
- **applications**: RNA knockdown, Diagnostics (SHERLOCK), Transient gene regulation
- **development stage**: mature
- **companies**: Sherlock Biosciences, Mammoth Biosciences
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Cytosine Base Editor (CBE)

- **name**: Cytosine Base Editor (CBE)
- **category**: gene_editing_tools
- **editing type**: base editing
- **mechanism**: Deaminase-fused Cas9 nickase converts C→T (G→A)
- **key features**: No double-strand breaks, Single nucleotide precision, C→T conversion
- **applications**: Point mutation correction, Stop codon introduction, Disease modeling
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Beam Therapeutics, Pairwise
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Adenine Base Editor (ABE)

- **name**: Adenine Base Editor (ABE)
- **category**: gene_editing_tools
- **editing type**: base editing
- **mechanism**: Deaminase-fused Cas9 nickase converts A→G (T→C)
- **key features**: No double-strand breaks, A→G conversion, Complementary to CBE
- **applications**: Point mutation correction, Sickle cell disease, Genetic disease therapy
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Beam Therapeutics, Verve Therapeutics
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Prime Editor (PE)

- **name**: Prime Editor (PE)
- **category**: gene_editing_tools
- **editing type**: prime editing
- **mechanism**: Cas9 nickase-reverse transcriptase fusion using pegRNA
- **key features**: Search-and-replace, All 12 point mutations, Small insertions/deletions, No DSB required
- **applications**: Precise gene correction, Insertions up to 50bp, Complex edits
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Prime Medicine
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### CRISPR Epigenome Editor (MEK)

- **name**: CRISPR Epigenome Editor (MEK)
- **category**: gene_editing_tools
- **editing type**: epigenetic activation
- **mechanism**: dCas9 fused to transcriptional activators removes repressive methylation marks (H3K9me3) to durably reactivate silenced genes without DNA cutting
- **key features**: No double-strand breaks, Durable gene reactivation, Multiplexed gene control, Transient delivery achieves sustained effect
- **applications**: Reactivating silenced tumor suppressor genes, Gene therapy for genetic silencing disorders, Epigenetic disease modification
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **companies**: Tune Therapeutics, Chroma Medicine
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high

#### CRISPR-Cas12a

- **name**: CRISPR-Cas12a
- **type**: Gene editing tool
- **mechanism**: Similar to Cas9, Cas12a uses a guide RNA to target specific DNA sequences and creates a double-strand break, but it has different PAM requirements and can process its own guide RNA.
- **target**: Specific DNA sequences for genetic modifications, enabling simultaneous assessment of multiple genetic changes.
- **advantage**: Allows for more seamless gene editing and improved disease modeling by enabling multiplexed edits.
- **status**: Emerging tool with applications in research and disease modeling.
- **description**: CRISPR-Cas12a is a new tool that helps researchers simultaneously assess the impact of multiple genetic changes involved in diseases. It offers a more seamless approach to gene editing compared to traditional Cas9. This tool is particularly useful for creating improved disease models and studying co...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://news.yale.edu/2025/03/20/new-crispr-tool-enables-more-seamless-gene-editing-and-improved-disease-modeling', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### Base Editors

- **name**: Base Editors
- **type**: Gene editing tool
- **mechanism**: Derived from the CRISPR-Cas system, base editors fuse a catalytically impaired Cas protein with a base-modifying enzyme to directly convert one base pair to another without requiring double-strand breaks.
- **target**: Specific point mutations in DNA to correct disease-causing single nucleotide variants.
- **advantage**: Enables precise point mutations without inducing double-strand breaks, reducing the risk of unwanted genomic alterations.
- **status**: Advanced technology with clinical applications, recognized by the 2025 Breakthrough Prize.
- **description**: Base editors are a novel genome editing technology developed to introduce specific point mutations without requiring DNA double-strand breaks. They are particularly useful for correcting single nucleotide variants that cause genetic diseases. The technology was recognized with the 2025 Breakthrough ...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.the-scientist.com/david-liu-wins-2025-breakthrough-prize-for-base-editing-and-prime-editing-72888', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://advance...

#### Prime Editors

- **name**: Prime Editors
- **type**: Gene editing tool
- **mechanism**: Prime editing uses a Cas9 nickase fused to a reverse transcriptase and a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA) to directly write new genetic information into a specified DNA site without requiring double-strand breaks or donor templates.
- **target**: Precise genetic modifications, including insertions, deletions, and all base-to-base conversions.
- **advantage**: Enables precise genome editing without double-strand breaks, expanding the scope and capabilities of genetic modifications.
- **status**: Advanced technology with ongoing improvements for efficiency and broader applications.
- **description**: Prime editing is an advanced genome editing technology that can replace disease-causing sections of DNA with new segments without inducing double-strand breaks. It substantially expands the scope of genome editing and can correct the vast majority of known genetic variants. The technology is continu...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.broadinstitute.org/news/scientists-improve-nearly-every-aspect-prime-editing-moving-it-closer-treating-more-genetic', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url'...

#### Glybera

- **name**: Glybera
- **target disease**: Lipoprotein lipase deficiency
- **vector**: AAV
- **delivery method**: ex-vivo
- **status**: Approved (EMA)
- **description**: Glybera was the first gene therapy approved in Europe for the treatment of lipoprotein lipase deficiency, a rare inherited disease. It uses an AAV vector to deliver a functional copy of the LPL gene to patient cells, which are then reinfused. The therapy was approved by the EMA in 2012 but later wit...

#### Zolgensma

- **name**: Zolgensma
- **target disease**: Spinal muscular atrophy
- **vector**: AAV
- **delivery method**: in-vivo
- **status**: Approved (FDA, EMA)
- **description**: Zolgensma is a one-time AAV9-based gene therapy designed to treat spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), a rare and often fatal genetic disease that causes muscle weakness and atrophy. It delivers a functional copy of the SMN1 gene directly to motor neurons, addressing the genetic root cause of the disease....
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.fda.gov/vaccines-blood-biologics/cellular-gene-therapy-products/approved-cellular-and-gene-therapy-products', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### Kymriah

- **name**: Kymriah
- **target disease**: B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia
- **vector**: Lentivirus
- **delivery method**: ex-vivo
- **status**: Approved (FDA)
- **description**: Kymriah is a CAR-T cell therapy, a type of ex-vivo gene therapy, that uses a lentiviral vector to genetically modify a patient's own T cells to express a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR). This engineered CAR-T cell is then infused back into the patient to target and eliminate CD19-positive cancer cel...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.fda.gov/vaccines-blood-biologics/cellular-gene-therapy-products/approved-cellular-and-gene-therapy-products', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

- **delivery method**: in-vivo
- **status**: Approved (FDA)
- **description**: Luxturna is a voretigene neparvovec, an AAV-based gene therapy used to treat inherited retinal dystrophy caused by mutations in the RPE65 gene. It is administered via a subretinal injection to deliver a functional copy of the RPE65 gene directly to retinal cells, restoring their ability to produce a...

- **description**: EBT-101, developed by Excision Biotherapeutics, is a first-in-class CRISPR-based gene-editing therapy designed as a functional cure for HIV. #### Elevidys (delandistrogene moxeparvovec) — DMD Gene Therapy

- **name**: Elevidys (delandistrogene moxeparvovec) — DMD Gene Therapy
- **target genes**: DMD (dystrophin
- **target diseases**: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy
- **therapy type**: AAV_delivery
- **therapy types**: AAV_delivery
- **development stage**: approved
- **companies**: Sarepta Therapeutics, Roche
- **vectors**: AAVrh74
- **mechanism**: Delivers a micro-dystrophin gene via AAVrh74 vector to muscle cells, producing a shortened but functional dystrophin protein
- **key findings**: FDA expanded approval in June 2024 to cover all DMD mutations regardless of mutation type, Accelerated approval converted to traditional approval based on functional improvement data, Phase 3 EMBARK trial showed significant improvement in NSAA functional scores, One-time IV infusion providing long-t...
- **approval date**: 2023-06-22
- **approval agency**: FDA
- **sources**: FDA, Sarepta Therapeutics
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Beqvez (fidanacogene elaparvovec) — Hemophilia B Gene Therapy

- **name**: Beqvez (fidanacogene elaparvovec) — Hemophilia B Gene Therapy
- **target genes**: F9 (Factor IX
- **target diseases**: Hemophilia B
- **therapy type**: AAV_delivery
- **therapy types**: AAV_delivery
- **development stage**: approved
- **companies**: Pfizer
- **vectors**: AAVrh74var
- **mechanism**: Delivers a high-activity Factor IX Padua gene variant via AAVrh74 vector to liver cells for sustained FIX production
- **key findings**: FDA approved April 2024 for adults with hemophilia B, BeneFix trial showed 71% reduction in annualized bleeding rate, Factor IX activity sustained at mean 39.0 IU/dL at 2 years post-treatment, One-time IV infusion replacing lifelong Factor IX injections
- **approval date**: 2024-04-26
- **approval agency**: FDA
- **sources**: FDA, Pfizer, NEJM
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Kebilidi (elapargene) — AADC Deficiency Gene Therapy

- **name**: Kebilidi (elapargene) — AADC Deficiency Gene Therapy
- **target genes**: DDC (DOPA decarboxylase
- **target diseases**: Aromatic L-Amino Acid Decarboxylase Deficiency
- **therapy type**: AAV_delivery
- **therapy types**: AAV_delivery
- **development stage**: approved
- **companies**: PTC Therapeutics
- **vectors**: AAV2
- **mechanism**: First intrathecally administered gene therapy, delivering functional DDC gene directly to the CNS via AAV2 vector
- **key findings**: FDA approved July 2024 — first gene therapy delivered via intrathecal injection, First approved treatment for AADC deficiency, a rare neurotransmitter disorder, Clinical trial showed improved motor function and cognitive development in pediatric patients, Direct CNS delivery bypasses blood-brain bar...
- **approval date**: 2024-07-24
- **approval agency**: FDA
- **sources**: FDA, PTC Therapeutics
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Vyjuvek (beremagene geperpavec) — DEB Topical Gene Therapy

- **name**: Vyjuvek (beremagene geperpavec) — DEB Topical Gene Therapy
- **target genes**: COL7A1
- **target diseases**: Dystrophic Epidermolysis Bullosa
- **therapy type**: AAV_delivery
- **therapy types**: AAV_delivery
- **development stage**: approved
- **companies**: Krystal Biotech
- **vectors**: HSV-1 (modified herpes simplex
- **mechanism**: First topical gene therapy — delivers functional COL7A1 gene via modified HSV-1 vector applied directly to skin wounds
- **key findings**: FDA approved May 2023 — first topical gene therapy ever approved, Reduces wound area by 67% versus placebo in Phase 3 GEM-3 trial, Non-invasive gel application enabling repeat dosing, Addresses both visible and invisible wounds across entire body surface
- **approval date**: 2023-05-19
- **approval agency**: FDA
- **sources**: FDA, Krystal Biotech, Nature Medicine
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Adstiladrin (nadofaragene firadenovec) — Bladder Cancer Gene Therapy

- **name**: Adstiladrin (nadofaragene firadenovec) — Bladder Cancer Gene Therapy
- **target genes**: IFNA2 (Interferon alpha-2b)
- **target diseases**: BCG-Unresponsive Non-Muscle Invasive Bladder Cancer
- **therapy type**: non-replicating viral vector
- **therapy types**: adenoviral_vector, intravesical
- **development stage**: approved
- **companies**: Ferring Pharmaceuticals
- **vectors**: non-replicating adenovirus
- **mechanism**: Intravesical delivery of adenoviral vector encoding IFNα2b directly into bladder, causing local cells to produce interferon for anti-tumor immunity
- **key findings**: FDA approved December 2022 for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC with CIS, Phase 3 CS-003 trial: 53.4% complete response rate at 3 months, Durable response: 45.5% maintained CR at 12 months, First intravesical gene therapy approved in the US
- **approval date**: 2022-12-16
- **approval agency**: FDA
- **sources**: FDA, Ferring Pharmaceuticals, JCO
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Severe Combined Immunodeficiency

- **name**: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency
- **symbol**: IL2RG
- **chromosome**: X
- **function**: Encodes the common gamma chain subunit of interleukin receptors, essential for immune cell development and function.
- **associated diseases**: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID)
- **description**: SCID, often called 'bubble boy disease,' is a genetic disorder characterized by a profound lack of immune function, leaving individuals highly susceptible to infections. The IL2RG gene mutation is a common cause of X-linked SCID, disrupting the development of T, B, and NK cells. Gene therapy targeti...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://innovativegenomics.org/news/crispr-clinical-trials-2026', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### CancerPAM

- **name**: CancerPAM
- **symbol**: N/A
- **chromosome**: N/A
- **function**: A multi-omics pipeline for identifying patient-specific, tumour-exclusive CRISPR-Cas9 knock-in sites for pro-inflammatory therapy.
- **associated diseases**: Cancer
- **description**: CancerPAM is an advanced computational framework designed to personalize cancer gene therapy. It analyzes a patient's unique tumor genetics to find safe and effective locations for inserting therapeutic genes. This approach aims to enhance the body's immune response to cancer while minimizing off-ta...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://crisprmedicinenews.com/news/cmn-weekly-12-december-2025-your-weekly-crispr-medicine-news', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### Ultra-rare Gene-based Therapy

- **name**: Ultra-rare Gene-based Therapy
- **symbol**: N/A
- **chromosome**: N/A
- **function**: A network providing funding and resources to advance gene-based therapies for ultra-rare neurological diseases.
- **associated diseases**: Ultra-rare Neurological Diseases
- **description**: The URGenT network focuses on developing and delivering gene therapies for patient populations with extremely rare genetic neurological conditions. It coordinates research and clinical efforts to overcome the unique challenges of treating these diseases. This initiative represents a targeted approac...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.ninds.nih.gov/current-research/research-funded-ninds/translational-research/ultra-rare-gene-based-therapy-urgent-network', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### N-of-1 Trials

- **name**: N-of-1 Trials
- **symbol**: N/A
- **chromosome**: N/A
- **function**: A clinical trial design for a single patient, used for individualized, gene-targeted therapies.
- **associated diseases**: Rare Genetic Disorders
- **description**: N-of-1 trials are a research methodology where a single patient is the entire study population, allowing for highly personalized treatment plans. This approach is particularly valuable for ultra-rare diseases where traditional trials are not feasible. It is a key component of the FDA's new pathway f...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-54077-5', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.clinicaltrialsarena.com/news/gene-editing-takes-centre-stage-i...

#### CRISPR-Cas9

- **name**: CRISPR-Cas9
- **symbol**: N/A
- **chromosome**: N/A
- **function**: A gene-editing tool derived from a bacterial immune system, used for making precise changes to DNA.
- **associated diseases**: Various Genetic Diseases
- **description**: CRISPR-Cas9 is a revolutionary technology that allows scientists to edit parts of the genome by removing, adding, or altering sections of the DNA sequence. It has evolved from its natural role in bacteria into a powerful tool for research and therapy. Its application is central to many current clini...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12503735', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### Gene Therapy

- **name**: Gene Therapy
- **symbol**: N/A
- **chromosome**: N/A
- **function**: A medical field that uses genes to treat or prevent disease, often by delivering functional genes to replace faulty ones.
- **associated diseases**: Rare Diseases, Genetic Disorders
- **description**: Gene therapy involves modifying the genetic material within a patient's cells to treat or cure disease. It holds immense promise for conditions that currently have no effective treatments, particularly rare genetic disorders. Recent advances have led to its increased use in clinical practice and reg...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd8QRswtk8o', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### CRISPR Screening

- **name**: CRISPR Screening
- **symbol**: N/A
- **chromosome**: N/A
- **function**: A functional genomics method using CRISPR to systematically knock out or activate genes to identify their function.
- **associated diseases**: Cancer, Various Diseases
- **description**: CRISPR screening is a powerful tool that allows researchers to test the function of thousands of genes at once in a cell. By systematically disabling genes, scientists can identify which ones are critical for cell survival or disease progression. This method is crucial for discovering new therapeuti...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2095177925001741', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### CRISPR Gene Editing Market

- **name**: CRISPR Gene Editing Market
- **symbol**: N/A
- **chromosome**: N/A
- **function**: The commercial sector focused on the development and application of CRISPR-based gene editing technologies.
- **associated diseases**: Various Diseases
- **description**: The CRISPR gene editing market is a rapidly growing industry driven by the therapeutic potential of gene editing technologies. It is projected to grow significantly as more treatments move from research to clinical application. This market encompasses companies developing tools, therapies, and suppo...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.towardshealthcare.com/insights/crispr-gene-editing-market-sizing', 'collected_at': '2026-06-19T08:00:00Z'}

#### SGSH (N-sulfoglucosamine sulfohydrolase)

- **name**: SGSH (N-sulfoglucosamine sulfohydrolase)
- **symbol**: SGSH
- **chromosome**: 17q25.3
- **function**: Lysosomal enzyme that degrades heparan sulfate in the glycosaminoglycan breakdown pathway
- **associated diseases**: Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A (MPS IIIA), Mucopolysaccharidosis IIIA
- **description**: The SGSH gene encodes N-sulfoglucosamine sulfohydrolase, a lysosomal enzyme essential for the stepwise degradation of heparan sulfate, a glycosaminoglycan found in the extracellular matrix. Mutations in SGSH cause Sanfilippo Syndrome Type A (MPS IIIA), a devastating autosomal recessive neurodegenera...
- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://www.cgtlive.com/view/ultragenyx-resubmits-bla-ux111-sanfilippo-syndrome', 'collected_at': '2026-06-22T06:40:00Z'}

### genomic diagnostics

#### Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)

- **name**: Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: sequencing
- **mechanism**: Massively parallel sequencing of DNA fragments
- **key features**: High throughput, Cost decreasing rapidly, Multiple platforms available
- **applications**: Gene panel testing, Whole exome sequencing, Whole genome sequencing, Transcriptomics
- **market size**: NGS market projected significant growth 2026-2035
- **companies**: Illumina, Thermo Fisher, Oxford Nanopore, Pacific Biosciences
- **development stage**: clinical
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Liquid Biopsy

- **name**: Liquid Biopsy
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: diagnostics
- **mechanism**: Detection of circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA), circulating tumor cells, and exosomes in blood
- **key features**: Non-invasive, Real-time monitoring, Early detection potential, Minimal sample required
- **applications**: Cancer early detection, Treatment monitoring, Minimal residual disease, Mutation tracking
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Guardant Health, Grail (Illumina), Foundation Medicine, Roche
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS)

- **name**: Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS)
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: sequencing
- **mechanism**: Complete sequencing of an organism's entire genome
- **key features**: Comprehensive coverage, Non-coding region analysis, Structural variant detection
- **applications**: Rare disease diagnosis, Cancer genomics, Pharmacogenomics, Population genomics
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Illumina, Pacific Biosciences, Oxford Nanopore
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Whole Exome Sequencing (WES)

- **name**: Whole Exome Sequencing (WES)
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: sequencing
- **mechanism**: Sequencing of all protein-coding regions (~1-2% of genome)
- **key features**: Cost-effective vs WGS, Captures most disease-causing variants, Clinical utility established
- **applications**: Rare disease diagnosis, Cancer mutation profiling, Genetic disorder screening
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Illumina, Thermo Fisher
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Targeted Gene Panels

- **name**: Targeted Gene Panels
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: sequencing
- **mechanism**: NGS-based targeted sequencing of selected gene sets
- **key features**: High depth coverage, Cost-effective, Fast turnaround, Specific disease focus
- **applications**: Hereditary cancer screening, Cardiogenomics, Pharmacogenomics
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Invitae, Myriad Genetics, Foundation Medicine
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Oxford Nanopore Sequencing

- **name**: Oxford Nanopore Sequencing
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: sequencing
- **mechanism**: Real-time sequencing via protein nanopores detecting DNA strand current changes
- **key features**: Real-time results, Long reads (>10kb), Portable (MinION), Direct RNA sequencing
- **applications**: Field diagnostics, Infectious disease, Structural variant detection, Epigenetic detection
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Oxford Nanopore Technologies
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Pacific Biosciences HiFi Sequencing

- **name**: Pacific Biosciences HiFi Sequencing
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: sequencing
- **mechanism**: Single-molecule real-time (SMRT) sequencing with circular consensus
- **key features**: High accuracy (>99.9%), Long reads (15-25kb), No amplification bias
- **applications**: De novo genome assembly, Structural variant detection, Haplotype-resolved sequencing
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Pacific Biosciences
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

- **technology**: Genetic testing
- **application**: Personalized medicine and drug response prediction
- **cost**: Variable, depends on the test
- **status**: Established
- **description**: Pharmacogenomics involves studying how genes affect a person's response to drugs. It helps in predicting which medications will be most effective and which may cause adverse reactions. This field is integral to personalized medicine, optimizing treatment plans based on individual genetic profiles.

#### Spatial Transcriptomics

- **name**: Spatial Transcriptomics
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: spatial omics
- **mechanism**: Maps gene expression patterns to tissue spatial coordinates, preserving geographical information lost in single-cell dissociation. Technologies include 10x Visium, MERFISH, Stereo-seq, and CosMx
- **key features**: Resolves gene expression in 2D/3D tissue context, Sub-cellular resolution (Stereo-seq, CosMx), Can profile 1000+ genes simultaneously, Reveals tumor microenvironment architecture
- **applications**: Tumor microenvironment profiling, Developmental biology spatial atlases, Drug response heterogeneity, Neuroscience brain mapping
- **advantages**: Spatial context preserved, Multi-gene profiling, Clinical pathology integration
- **limitations**: High cost per sample, Data analysis complexity, Sample preparation challenges
- **companies**: 10x Genomics, Vizgen, NanoString, BGI
- **development stage**: clinical
- **sources**: Nature Biotechnology, 10x Genomics, Cell
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Long-Read Sequencing (PacBio Revio, Oxford Nanopore)

- **name**: Long-Read Sequencing (PacBio Revio, Oxford Nanopore)
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: sequencing
- **mechanism**: Single-molecule sequencing generating reads >10kb, resolving repetitive regions, structural variants, and epigenetic modifications that short-read NGS cannot detect
- **key features**: Read lengths 10-100kb+ (vs 150-300bp for NGS), Direct detection of DNA methylation without bisulfite, Resolves complex structural variants, Full-length transcript isoform sequencing (Iso-Seq)
- **applications**: Structural variant detection in genetic diseases, Repeat expansion disorders (Huntington, Fragile X), HLA typing for transplantation, Microbial genome assembly
- **advantages**: Resolves repeats and SVs, Native methylation detection, Full-length transcripts
- **limitations**: Higher per-base cost than NGS, Lower throughput (improving), Higher error rate (improving)
- **companies**: Pacific Biosciences, Oxford Nanopore Technologies
- **development stage**: clinical
- **sources**: Nature Reviews Genetics, PacBio, Oxford Nanopore
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### AI-Powered Variant Interpretation

- **name**: AI-Powered Variant Interpretation
- **category**: genomic_diagnostics
- **technology type**: AI/ML diagnostics
- **mechanism**: Deep learning models (DeepVariant, SEQUIA, AlphaMissense) that classify genomic variants of uncertain significance (VUS) by integrating genomic, transcriptomic, and clinical data
- **key features**: DeepVariant: Google's CNN-based variant caller with >99.99% accuracy, AlphaMissense: DeepMind's model predicting pathogenicity of all 71M missense variants, SEQUIA: Ensemble approach integrating multiple AI models, Reduces VUS rate from ~30% to <10% in clinical genomics
- **applications**: Clinical genomic testing interpretation, Rare disease diagnosis, Pharmacogenomics, Population screening
- **advantages**: Scalable, Reduces VUS burden, Continuously improving
- **limitations**: Training data bias, Black-box interpretability, Needs clinical validation
- **companies**: Google DeepMind, Illumina, Congenica, Invitae
- **development stage**: clinical
- **sources**: Science (AlphaMissense, Nature Biotechnology, Google DeepMind
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

### main

### regenerative medicine

#### Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)

- **name**: Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: stem cell
- **mechanism**: Reprogramming adult cells to pluripotent state via Yamanaka factors
- **key features**: Patient-specific cells, Avoids ethical concerns of ESCs, Unlimited self-renewal, Differentiation to any cell type
- **applications**: Disease modeling, Drug screening, Cell therapy, Personalized medicine
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Fujifilm Cellular Dynamics, Allele Biotech, CDI Biosciences
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs)

- **name**: Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs)
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: stem cell
- **mechanism**: Adult stem cells with immunomodulatory and regenerative properties
- **key features**: Immunomodulatory, Paracrine signaling, Multiple tissue sources, Low immunogenicity
- **applications**: Orthopedic repair, Autoimmune disease, Graft-versus-host disease, Inflammatory conditions
- **development stage**: clinical
- **companies**: Mesoblast, Pluristem
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### MSC-Derived Exosomes

- **name**: MSC-Derived Exosomes
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: cell-free therapy
- **mechanism**: Extracellular vesicles from MSCs carrying therapeutic cargo
- **key features**: Cell-free approach, Paracrine mechanism, Lower safety concerns than cells, Scalable production
- **applications**: Tissue repair, Anti-inflammatory therapy, Wound healing, Neuroprotection
- **development stage**: preclinical/clinical
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: medium
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

#### Organoids

- **name**: Organoids
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: tissue engineering
- **mechanism**: 3D self-organizing tissue cultures from stem cells mimicking organ structure
- **key features**: 3D organ-like structure, Patient-specific, Drug testing platform, Disease modeling
- **applications**: Drug discovery, Disease modeling, Personalized therapy testing, Developmental biology
- **development stage**: research/preclinical
- **companies**: HUB Organoids, Emulate
- **sources**: web_search
- **confidence**: high
- **first seen**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z
- **last updated**: 2026-05-31T15:38:09.017Z

- **sources**: {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'article_url': 'https://scienceblog.cincinnatichildrens.org/organoid-production-breakthrough-to-help-accelerate-disease-and-drug-development-research', 'collected_at': '2026-06-22T06:40:00Z'}, {'source_type': 'web', 'source_credibility': 'B', 'articl...

#### Synthetic Embryo Models (SEM-2025)

- **name**: Synthetic Embryo Models (SEM-2025)
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: stem cell
- **mechanism**: Self-organizing 3D structures derived from pluripotent stem cells that recapitulate key developmental stages of mammalian embryogenesis without sperm or egg
- **key features**: Models post-implantation development inaccessible to natural embryo studies, Ethical advantage: no fertilization or totipotency required, Enables drug screening for teratogenicity, Can model early organogenesis (neurulation, cardiogenesis)
- **applications**: Developmental biology research, Teratogenicity drug screening, Infertility research, Organogenesis modeling
- **advantages**: No ethical concerns of natural embryos, Scalable production, Human-specific developmental insights
- **limitations**: Incomplete recapitulation, Lack of extra-embryonic tissues, Regulatory uncertainty
- **companies**: Academic research, Renature Bio
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **sources**: Cell, Nature, Science (2024-2025 ISSCR guidelines
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### 3D Bioprinted Functional Cardiac Patches

- **name**: 3D Bioprinted Functional Cardiac Patches
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: tissue engineering
- **mechanism**: Bioprinting patient-derived iPSC-cardiomyocytes in fibrin/alginate hydrogel scaffolds to create contractile cardiac patches for myocardial repair
- **key features**: Patient-specific cells reduce immune rejection, Synchronized contraction with host myocardium, Vascularization strategies integrated into printed constructs, Can be scaled to clinically relevant sizes
- **applications**: Post-myocardial infarction repair, Drug cardiotoxicity screening, Heart failure tissue engineering
- **advantages**: Patient-specific, Contractile function, Integration with host tissue
- **limitations**: Vascularization challenges, Long-term survival, Regulatory pathway unclear
- **companies**: Biolife4D, Cellink, 3D Bioprinting Solutions
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **sources**: Nature Biotechnology, Advanced Science, Tissue Engineering
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### iPSC-Derived Pancreatic Islets for Diabetes

- **name**: iPSC-Derived Pancreatic Islets for Diabetes
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: stem cell therapy
- **mechanism**: Differentiation of iPSCs into functional pancreatic beta cells and islet organoids capable of glucose-responsive insulin secretion, for transplantation into Type 1 diabetes patients
- **key features**: Glucose-stimulated insulin secretion comparable to primary islets, Scalable production from patient-specific iPSCs, Can be encapsulated for immune protection, Eliminates donor pancreas dependency
- **applications**: Type 1 diabetes cell therapy, Diabetes drug screening, Islet biology research
- **advantages**: Unlimited supply, Patient-specific immunocompatibility, Glucose-responsive
- **limitations**: Immune encapsulation challenges, Long-term maturation needed, Cost of iPSC manufacturing
- **companies**: Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VX-880, Semma Therapeutics, ViaCyte
- **development stage**: clinical
- **sources**: NEJM, Nature, Cell Stem Cell, ADA 2024
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25

#### Organoid Intelligence (OI) — Brain Organoid Computing

- **name**: Organoid Intelligence (OI) — Brain Organoid Computing
- **category**: regenerative_medicine
- **technology type**: organoid
- **mechanism**: Brain organoids derived from human iPSCs grown into self-organized 3D neural networks capable of learning and basic information processing, interfaced with electrode arrays
- **key features**: Human neural network with 800+ neurons per organoid, Demonstrated basic learning (Pong game task), MEA (multi-electrode array) interface for I/O, Biological computing substrate
- **applications**: Biological computing systems, Neurotoxicity screening, Neurodevelopmental disease modeling, AI-hybrid intelligence systems
- **advantages**: Human neural architecture, Low energy computing, Plasticity and learning
- **limitations**: Limited lifespan, No vascularization, Ethical considerations, Scalability
- **companies**: Johns Hopkins University, Cortical Labs
- **development stage**: preclinical
- **sources**: Frontiers in Science (2024, Cell, Johns Hopkins
- **confidence**: high
- **date added**: 2026-06-25
